CURRENT AFFAIRS.

Tt is no wonder there is dissatisfaction on the Great
ourface Railway, and that the imported Mr. Dent
is most unpopular. The treatment of Mr. Patk. Deering
by the railway company is so bad that many people
ould at first regard it as incredible. M. "Deering was
r twenty years in the service of the company and
period of eleven years was head porter at Roscrea.

is a life-long teetotaller, and during all his service
ith the railway company there never was a charge nor

a complaint against him. M. Deering played an heroic
art on the occasion of the terrible Roscrea accident.
7 of our readers may remember that when the
riages moved on on that unfortunate occasion Mr.
atrick Deering, who was head porter, rushed after
i d succeeded in boarding the brake van.

was not his post so that he was really
he remained on the platform he

d ¢ & eroic part and he probably
oscrea to-day. But he did

the plucky thing, and ran after the frain for about a
quarter of a mile and successfully boarded it and worked

- the brake for all it was worth. Had he not done so

the consequences of the accident might have been far
blacker. The brakes were not equal to stopping the
train, but at least Mr. Deering had rushed to the rescue
and had done his best. The immediate result for Mr.
Deering was that his nerves were shattered and his
muscles strained, and one would think that one of the
inevitable questions was—how much compensation
should he receive ? But Mr. Patrick Deering did not
claim any compensation.  Shattered in nerve and
strained in muscle though he was as a result of the
accident which he did his best to avert, he got to his
work very shortly again and did not lie up. S

Some time after the accident, the carriages were
{ested to see if it were possible to stop the runaway
train. But in this case empty carriages were used with
three brake vans, while to the train containing between
500 and 600 passengers there was attached only one
brake van (which was probably defective). Mr. Patrick
Deering called the attention of the Traffic Manager to
this unfair comparison, and no doubt the fact that he
did so helped to confirm the impending sentence of
banishment.

In due time Mr. Deering instead of receiving com-
pensation or promotion—was reduced! He was re-
duced and transferred to Pallas, where he had to work
seven days a week ab an under-staffed station. Here
1e was twice laid up on the sick list. After six months
at Pallas he was transferred to Tuam, and though he
1ad: no house to go to—we believe it is next to impos-
sible to get a house in Tuam—he was ordered to take -
1is furniture with him from Pallas. Eventually he got
some sort of a thatched cottage in Tuam, a dark, damp
cabin which let down the rain by the roof, and faced his
ward lot as best he could—he was experiencing the
wages for long and honourable service and for heroism
as doled out by the management of the Great Sourface
Railway. Unsuccessfully he asked to be transferred to
any town in Munster where he could get a house and
wheére his wife would have an opportunity of utilising
her certificated teaching ability. At last Mrs. Deering
obtained a very good position as teacher in Roscrea and
Mr. Deering applied to be changed back to Roscrea.
We understand that Mr. Neale of the Great Sourface
tailway was agreeable to this vequest, and why would
he not be so? Here was a man of twenty years’ un-
blemished service, whose health. was injured in his
brave attempt to avert the Roscrea accident, and having
been reduced and sent hither and thither for over a year,
at last his wife obtains a position in Roscrea and he
raquests the company to transfer him back to that town
with which he was so long connected and where he has
the good will and respect of all classes of the commu-
nity. Mr. Neale of the railway was, it is said, in
favour of granting the application, but the imported
My, Dent put down his foot. We think our readers
will agree with us that the treatment accorded to Mr.
Deering was all along cruel and unjust. On the face
of it he should be claiming compensation rather thait
requesbing small favours from the heartless and petby
tyrannical management. Mr. Deering is & quiet, ob-
liging, and transparently honest man. He now did a
thing which he should not have done, and so played
into the hands of the railway management. He should
have consulted a solicitor before he tool e step; but
he didn’t. Harried and harrassed by t net
he foolishly resigned his positil Den

So respected was and is Mr. Deering in Roscrea and
50 unexpected was his reduction and transfer from
Roscrea—for there was no charge against him—that
the chief traders and residents of Roscrea and Tulla-
more sent in a: public protest to. the railway company
against histransfer and reduction. But what does the
Great Sourface Railway care for the public? M.
Deering, unable to put up with his treatment, resigned
on March Sth last after 21 years of service. On June
3rd he wrote to the manager setting out his case and
asking for a personal interview. On June 7th the
general manager wrote that ‘“ T do not see any neces-
sity for the interview which you suggest, having regard
to the fact that you resigned from the company’s ser-
vice of your own accord.” Mr. Deering veplied on
June 11th and denied that he resigned of his own
accord. ‘“ On the contrary I did so most unwillingly,
and but for the pressure of circumstances, for which
the company alone was responsible, I would be in the
service of the railway yet.”  Of course the general
manager was technically - right in stating that M.
Deering had resigned of his own accord, even though
our readers will have concluded that he was hunted
down and driven to resignation. It is unfortunate that
he did not take legal advice before being goaded into
handing in his resignation. ‘‘ During my eleven years
as head porter in Roserea,”” said Mr.: Deering in the
second letter to the general-manager asking for an in-
terview, ** no complaints were made either of the plat-
form or the goods store. Consequently I could not
think it possible that I would be penalised for the rest
of my life, and so I remained in the service in spite of
the many indignities put upon me.””  On the 14th June
the general manager wrote enclosing a pass to Dublin.
Mi. Deering was in Dublin on Thursday of last week
and had an interview with two officials,- and subse-
quently with the grand pasha Dent himself. The pasha
played the card that Mr. Deering had resigned of his
own accord. And so after twenty-one years of service
this is the end of Mr. Deering’s railway career. He
was goaded into resignation, and he has no redress. TIs
it any wonder in view of this case that there is discon-
tent amongst the men on this line ?

- Mr. Deering is now living in Roscrea, popular and-:
respected by all classes, for a man who hed been a
Head Porter for eleven years is one of the best known
men in a town, and when he is thoroughly. honest,
obliging, industrious, and good-natured, he is bound to
be popular with. all classes. He has been treated dis-
gracefully by the Great. Sourface Railway; but people
lived iu Ireland before Brother Goulding was born or
before Dent was imported, and we are sure that Mr.
Deering will find that he can live and moderately
thrive in Roscrea despite all the petty persecution of
the railway company. We wish Mr. Deering good luck
and prosperity, and we hope to shortly hear that he is
doing better than he would ever have done had he
remained in the service of the Great Sourface Railway.

In Oireachtas week M. H. Gill and Son, Ltd., will
publish for Fr. Dinneen a concise English-Irish Dic-
tionary, containing some ten thousand English wor
and many thousand English phrases with their Iri
equivalents, all at the price of one shilling.  One is
accustomed to see English and foreign dictionaries
quoted at low prices. This is the first time that an
Irish dictionary of any sort has been announced at a
really popular price. ~Fr. Dinneen seems determined
to do for Emglish-Irish lexicography what he has already

~ so successfully achieved for Irish-English. The public
have long become familiar with his larger and smaller
Trish-English dictionary, the former of which being the
first to see the light. This time the order is-reversed.
The ““ Concise English-Irish Dictionary ’’ cannot fail to
prove of immense benefit to struggling learners and to
readers and writers of Irish of every class, and is issued
at a price which will bring it within reach of even the
pupils in all the National schools in Ireland where the
Trish language has secured a footing.

PHILIP THE SECOND OF SPAIN.

Hail, brooding Cheops of the Escorial,
Thy pyramid with primal strength devised
For a world wonder! hugeness solemnised
By art ascetic clouding like a pall
In grandiose reserve thy genius, all
Thy artists, councillors, thy monks here prized
\What time were priests and ovders civilized
'o dower the Church with art majesticall.
Great Catholic | strong king, o’er home, o’er clime
Afar from thy bare cell where thou couldst hear
Dream organ gusts 'mid liturgy severe, .
Or sad Vittoria weave voice chords sublime,
Hail! Thou did’st keep, with Culture’s cold disdain,
The vulgar Reformation out of Spain!

Under the heading * Trish v. English‘Poetry,” Mr.
Arthur Owen writes :—

Sir,—TFor some months I have read every line in
the LeaDER with great interest and pleasure. I admire
its sentiments and its fine literary touch.

May I, as a friend in feeling, and not in any carping
spirit, say I disagree with your contributor, ** Lagan ™’
on the subject of poetry ? " At the end of an interesting
articie the writer quotes—

““In deep valleyed Desmond a thousand bright foun-

Come down to that lake from

lanan) were Byron for all his Alping, and Slelley ‘for
all his sky-larking.” I rubbed my eyes, fearing like
Silas Wag, ‘‘ that my eyes deceived me, Mr. Boffin'!”’
Surely ‘‘ Lagan” was not serious when he matched
so to speak, the above lines with, I might say, ANy Two
lines of Byron or of Shelley? Thinking my judg-
ment of the lines was due to mental obscurity, T asked
the opinion of two friends of mine. One is an Oxford
M.A.; the other a former scholar of Bala College,
Wales.  Both were of opinion that the lines were not
to be compared with lines from the two poets named. .
I have read, and had my blood stirred and my pulse
quickened by many beautiful Irish poems; and am
1. displaying my ignorance of the subject by
saying the gem of gems (to my mind) is Clarence Man-
gan’s *' My Dark Rosaleen "’ ? Another gem T remem-
ber is D’Arcy McGee's ‘‘ My Trish Wife.”

I trust you or ““Lagan’’ will favour me with an
illuminating reply to dispel my darkness. In con-
clusion, why is it that in a company composed for the
most parb of Trishmen, when the subject of poetry is
mentioned, Moore is always voted the greatest Roman
of them all ?

Yours very truly,
ArTHUR OWEN,

The great Ivish language question is how to save it

in the districts where it'is understood and spoken. The

work can be approached from many points. Bally-

. ferriter, in the extreme south-west of Kerry, is often
called the next parish to America. A local correspon-
dent sends us the following interesting remarks on a
mission recently conducted there by two Capuchin
Fathers :—'‘ The week that has just expired was one
of the best and most happy weeks we ever have had in
Ballyferriter. A mission was preached in the parish
church by two Capuchin Fathers—Fathers Augustine
and Albert—and the great outstanding feature of the

. mission was that all the sermons, lectures and hymns
were in Irish. There was not so much as one English
sentence spoken from the altar. All the old people
were delighted, and great enthusiasm prevails amongst
the young, whose language is the Irish still. As far as
we know here, this is the first entirely Irish mission
that has been given in the south of Ireland, and per-
haps even in the west and north there has been nothing
like it. We have often heard that Ballyvourney is the
capital of Irish Ireland, and Ballingeary is known far
and wide as the home of Munster Training College,
but we are not aware that either of these places has
yet had an Irish mission. Therefore we claim to be
the pioneers in this matter, and that Ballyferriter,
where young and old speak the language, is the capital
of Irish Ireland. The credit of this Gaelic mission is
due to our earnest parish priest, the Rev. T. Griffin,
who is one of the best Irish Ireland workers in the whole
country. He and his genial curate preach in Irish
every Sunday, and we think there are few places in the
country now, alas, of which that can be said. Tathers
Augustine and Albert are working in Carrig Church in
this parish this week, and of course the work is being
done entirely in Irish. With young and old the mission
is the one topic of conversation, and we can safely say
that it has done more for the Irish language than any-
thing that has happened in Treland for many a long day.
Ballyferriter and its worthy priests have shown the way
to reach the hearts of Irish people in the Gaeldom.
Who will follow such noble example ?”’

The Skeffy gang we fear have con- *

hunger strikers should be released ** so as to put an end
to their torture.”” But the British Minister, by the
name of McKenna, promptly replied, “ I do not keep
them in torture. They have only to take their food.’
This hunger strike business appears to us a very childish
and silly thing. It is particularly childish. We once
heard of-a precocious child who partly succeeded in
intimidating his parents by threatening on various
occasions that he would pull his eyes out. Well, chil-
dren are children; but the ‘‘ hunger strikers ’’ wish to
pose as level-headed politicians! There are thousands
of people who would eat if they had anything to eat,
and the ‘‘ hunger strikers ’’ must appear extra ridicu-
lous to the really hungry women, of whom there are
unhappily far too many. The utter want of a sense of
humour and proportion amongst some of the Suffers is
very damaging to them. It may be remembered that
that whiskered pandour, Skeffy, had a vote in South
Dublin, and as Cotton was not up to the mark for him,
and as the other chap—we think it was a man named
Cooper—was likewise unsuitable, Skeffy looked on and
did not play his ‘‘ chaney’ in the game; in fact Skeffy
had a little vote and Skeffy didn’t use it.

It will be very hard for the Skeffy gang to keep up
being good ‘‘ copy’’ for the Press. So long as they are
good copy to us we will do our part to serve them out
in lighter vein for the amusement of our readers, and
we suppose other papers will likewise fall victims to the
temptation. We see that ‘Skeffy is publicly tilting at
Professor M. T. Kettle now. A pronouncement from
Cruso is somewhat overdue. Surely he cannot be an
ex-Suffer. .

The Insurance Act is a rather complicated affair.
There is a Section II., and there is, we believe, a Major
Tuge on top of it. That is nearly as much as we know
about it. Our readers are familiar now with the exis-
tence of the Irish Cutlery Company. Some men went
down on Tuesday—the day we go to press—and regis-
tered, or attempted to register, themselves, as cutlers.
But Section II., over which Major Fuge is head jobo-
crat, could not officially: recognise the existence pof
cutlers in Ireland! Cutlers evidently are not supposed
to exist in Ireland. About two months ago an official
from the War Office inspected the Irish Cutlery Co.’s
factory, yet Section IT. of the Insurance Act cannot,

* or at least does not, recognise the existence of cutlers

in Ireland. All the cutlers in the Dublin factory will
have to send over their names to England. As it is our
publishing day we have not time to go further into the
matter. Perhaps Major Fuge will explain, or in de-
fault of doing so, tell the public how the “* d”’ dropped
out of his name.

HALF-HOLIDAY HORRORS.

T was a wise measure to bestow a half-holiday on
those who toil in shops. Hailf a day is better than
none, and all work and no play makes dullards of Jack
and Jill. At the same time the community is not a
little upset by the new arrangement. It has produced
confusion in the city and very nearly starvation in the
suburbs. A time-table showing what shops are shut
at one p.m. on the different days of the week is fast
becoming a necessity. The early bird, of course, can
still secure the worm, but some birds are not so early
owing to various reasons, and for these the closing of
portals an hour after noon is fraught with inconveni-
ence. We will get into it, of course. Just at present,
however, the Shops Act is providing the public with
cccasions for profanity.

Everyone has his or her own grievance. Place aux
dames, let us first consider the housekeeper who de-
cides on a Wednesday to regale her household on
mutton. She staggers and, were this the mid-Victo-
rian epoch, would unquestionably swoon, on finding
shutters on the windows of the butcher’s shop. Too
late she realises that it is the day when the flesh of
oxen, sheep, or goats cannot be obtained after the first
hour. An entire change of menu has hastily to be
made. The vegetables suited for a piece of boiled or
roast will not harmonise with fowl or fish. Everything
is disarranged, and none but those charged with com-
missariat responsibilities can fully realise-all the incon-
veniences this unexpected happening may give rise to.
And finally, when ‘‘ the brute '’ returns hungering for
his evening meal, it is no pleasant task to inform-him
that ‘“ because of those wretched half-days’’ he must
put up with a makeshift dinner. Nor should ‘*the
brute’s ”’ point of view be lost sight of. Nothing is less
likely to prove conducive to peace than a dinner of
herbs when a stalled ox was expected.

““ The brute ’’ has his own special troubles on ac-
count of the Shops Act. Hitherto in the course of the
day’s labours he could snatch a moment to purchase
such articles of apparel as he might need, whenever
cpportunity served. Now he must consthntly bear in
mind ‘‘ the day that’s in it.”” Nay more, he must even
take thought for the morrow. Ties may not be bought
on such a day. Trousers are unattainable after one

p.m. on yet another. A drink after ten on a Saturday
night were more easily compassed than a hair-cut or
o shave on a Saturday afternoon. All these things are
a weaviness of the flesh to the busy man.  He must
needs schedule the most trivial items if he is to pre-
sent a clean and dapper appearance through the week.
An additienal strain has been imposed upon a mind
already sufficiently taxed in seeking to remember when
bills fall due. The settled habits of a life-time must be
altered to meet with the exigencies of the new situa-
tion. 7

Tven in that brief interval snatched in the eatly
afternoon for a hasty luncheon the Shops Act smites a
blow at his comfort. The ‘shining waiter or the smil-
ing waitress who has become habituated to him now
abandons him one day a week to the mercies of
strangers.  On that black day he finds himself, so to
speak, beginning all over again. The newspaper is no
longer concealed beside his seat. Alacrity and assi-
duity are alike lacking in his chance attendant. —The
little more—and how much it is—that goes to smooth
the process of digestion never on that dark day finds
itself done to his satisfaction. True, *‘ they also serve
who only stand and wait,” but where is the genial
greeting, the cheery smile, the merry jest, and the
quick retort that on other days lighten the monotony
of a solit repast ? Alas, the half-day has claimed
the faithful servitor, and respectful indifference replaces
familiar attentiveness.

The Shops Act has to some extent blighted, not only
the happiness of a section of the community, but the
very appearance even of our cities as well. An aspect
of semi-mourning is worn by our streets. Here and
there shutters are up on shops, as though the fell Ser-
geant or else the Suffragette had struck a blow. On
Wednesdays and Saturdays, especially, our thorough-
fares resemble the land of the lotus-eaters, where it
seemed always afternoon.  The rush and surge of
homing shop-hands, once only to be met with when the
Angelus had sounded for the last time, now manifests
itself, albeit in a less degree, at one p.m. The flancur
and the flancuse (if such a word there be), the gilded
youth, and the hobbled maiden, flee while it is yet
morn and leave the streets to the rejoicing workers.

1t surely is a shame to interfere with the convenience
of the idle-rich for the benefit of the lowly. May we
not suppose with good reason that this pampering of
the humbler classes will but serve to heighten and to
merease that Spirit of Unrest already, unhappily, so
rife in the labour world? We may find strange inno-
vations resulting from these radical measures. One
would not be surprised if even golf, that Royal and
ancient game, were to be taken up by the masses now
thyatv they are being given time in which to play it.
What a demoralising effect that would have on the
pastime of the patrician! Well has it been said that
the holidays of the lower orders are productive of in-
convenience to the upper classes. After all there is
nothing like WORK for keeping the humbler folk con-
tented with their lot. What with bank holidays, half-
days and strikes, the working classes have so.much

THE PRODUCTION OF WEALTH.

T"is well to recall first principles at the present time
when labour unrest threatens to interfere with
the production of all wealth. In the present state of
the civilised world there seem to be five separate things
necessary for the production of wealth.

First.—The material world, with its mineral, vege-
table and animal life.

Second.—The creative mind of man, which plans
and directs the production of wealth from the natural
products of the material world. All men have not
equal brains for this purpose. In this matter it is
evident God has not made all men equal. To some
He has given five talents and to others only one talent.

Third.—The strength of the muscle of man with suffi-
clent brain power to use it, which, as directed by the
creative mind of the most talented men, is necessary
for the production of wealth out of the products of the

Fourth.—Accumulated wealth, such as food, cloth-

which are necessary to keep the labouring men, and
the creative or directing men, alive, whilst they are
producing more wealth. This is called *‘ Capital.” Tt

“increases in the world as time goes on, except when

classes.

Fifth.—The State or government which men have
found to be necessary to keep order amongst them, to
keep them from following their evil passions which
tempt them to destroy each other, and to take from
each other the property or wealth already acquired.
The State or government, under the present condition

The cause of the present unrest is the jealousy be-
tween the three classes of human beings on whom the
production of wealth depend; namely, the labourers,
the men with creative minds, and the men who own
the accumulated wealth of the world, who are called
Capitalists. The men with creative minds and the
Capitalists are oftentimes the same individuals; when
they are not, they seem to be able to agree as to the
amount of remuneration each class is to get. It is
not so with the labourers, there seems to be a constant
feud between them and their employers. There have
always been clever men who use their brains to endea-
vour to persuade the labourers that they should own
all wealth. Some do this from the foolish, although
unselfish,. wish to, as it were, create a new human
nature. Like Lucifer they wish to equal the Almighty,
and to create a new world. Others do it from pure
selfishness, ' because they find it is an easy way to
acquire some of the accumulated wealth which the
labourers possess. The labourers, besides their strength
of muscle and brains, possess much accumulated
wealth, owing to their great number, although they
may possess little individually.

Our present civilisation has grown up whilst the State
left freedom to the individual man to acquire accumu-
lated wealth, by leaving to men freedom to use their
creative mind, their strength, and their powers of
saving what they have acquired and leaving it to others
on their death, whilst the State itself has preserved
order by preventing individuals from injuring the person
and property of other individuals, and preventing one
class from injuring other classes.

Socialists claim that they could create a more pros-
perous and a more highly civilised world by making
the State own all acquired wealth, by taking away all
freedom from the men with creative minds and the
men with strength and muscle, and by making them
all work for the State, which would then apportion the
wealth of the world amongst the human beings. The
objection to this system is that it could not be carried
out without first changing human nature. Men must
be willing to work as hard for the common good as they
do now for their individual good—this Socialists can-
not accomplish. TUnder a Socialistic State men with
creative minds would surely not use them to the same
extent as they do at present, because they would have
less incentive to do so, when they are not allowed to
accumulate property. Labourers again would only work,
as did the negro slaves, from fear of the whip. They
would not work hard, as they would not have the in-
centive of living more comfortably, of laying by a little
wealth for their old age, or of enabling their children
to get on in life.

The State must prevent the freedom, which is allowed
individuals, from injuring others, but, if it destroys that
freedom altogether, it will destroy the incentive to work
which is necessary to preserve the accumulated wealth
which at present exists and to create further wealth.
Although I believe that Socialism would destroy civili-
sation, I think the time has come to make changes in
the present system of remunerating labour. I think

TO THE MEMBERS,

The Committee of Management have much pleasure in submitting the Statement of Accounts and
Balance Sheet for the year ended 31st December, 1911, as certified by Mr. John Reilly, F.S.A.A., Public
Auditor by appointment of H.M. Treasury under the Friendly Societies Acts.  In accordance with the prac-
tice adopted in former Reports, a Summary of I'unds, Assets and Liabil to date has been prepared for the
information of the Members, and is appended, so as to indicate the Society’s present financial position.

PREMIUM INCOME.—The Premium Income amounted to £25,207 13s. 7d. as against £17,883 3s. 5d.
for 1910, an increase of £7,324 10s. 2d., or roughly 41 per cent. Satisfactory as this result is as an index of
progress and prosperity, the accession of revenue is still more remarkable when it is remembered that the
period under review was one of the most unfavourable seasons on record. Industrial unrest acted as a check
on all enterprise, and several other causes, notably the introduction of the National Insurance Bill, contri-
buted to the difficulties of canvassing, but we have more than held our own, thanks to the untiring energy.
perseverance, and loyalty of our staff in the face of determined and often unscrupulous opposition.

WORKING EXPENSES.—Notwithstanding the necessary extra outlay incidental to the expansion and
development of the Society’s business, and the large amount of new premiums entered, it has been possible
to keep the cost of management at the very low ratio of 40 per cent., though, as has been pointed out in
i previcus Reports, the expenses of even the oldest and best conducted Offices average about 45 per cent., and
| it is the custom in many young Companies to carry forward the bulk of the establishment, organisation and
i extension charges as an asset, representing money actually spent in preliminary work and in building up a
connection. In our case no credit is taken for these in the Balance Sheet; every item of expenditure and
depreciation has been written off against the year's income, as detailed in the accounts.

NATIONAL INSURANCE ACT.—The much-discussed State Insurance scheme, admittedly one of the
most complicated and far-reaching measures ever placed on the Statute-book, became law in December, 1911,
and, after careful examination of the measure and the clauses specially bearing on Friendly Societies’ opera-
tions, it was decided, in view of their probable effect, to recommend that steps be taken as soon as may be
to form a separate Approved Section for the transaction of business and the administration of benefits under
the Act. This departure will undoubtedly prove a valuable adjunct by reason of the additional facilities

afforded thereby to members and agents, and proper precautions are being inserted for the protection of their
rights and those of the Pavent Society.

O?FIGE ARRANGEMENTS, STAI‘F! ETC.—The requirements of the business have already out-grown

The relations between the Committee and the Officials of all grades have been most pleasant and har-
monious, and every member of the staff has done his share to maintain the Society’s position and prestige
and to advance its interests.
